era_context: The ancien régime in France is in severe financial crisis; King Louis XVI calls for the convening of the Estates-General (to meet in 1789). The U.S. Constitution has been drafted and is being ratified by the states. The Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792) is ongoing. In Britain, the Industrial Revolution is accelerating. In India, the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War is about to begin. The Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor is at the height of its power but beginning to face internal challenges.

events.title: U.S. Constitution ratified by nine states
events.description: The U.S. Constitution, drafted at the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia in 1787, was ratified by the ninth state (New Hampshire) on 21 June 1788, meeting the requirement for ratification. The Constitution established a federal system with separation of powers among executive, legislative, and judicial branches. The ratification process was contentious, with Federalists supporting the Constitution and Anti-Federalists opposing it as a threat to state sovereignty and individual liberty. The Federalist Papers, written by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay, were published during the ratification debates to argue for the Constitution's adoption. The Constitution's ratification marked a crucial moment in American history and established the framework for the federal government that would be inaugurated in 1789.
key_figures: James Madison (primary architect of the Constitution)
George Washington (president of the Constitutional Convention)
Anti-Federalist opponents (including Patrick Henry, George Mason)
events.certainty_note: The Constitution's ratification by nine states is precisely documented. The date (21 June 1788) is uncontested.

events.title: Louis XVI calls for the Estates-General
events.description: In August 1788, King Louis XVI, facing a severe financial crisis and mounting pressure from the nobility and the Third Estate, announced that he would convene the Estates-General in 1789. The Estates-General was an assembly of representatives from the three estates (clergy, nobility, and commoners) that had not met since 1614. The decision to convene the Estates-General was a major concession by the king and reflected the depth of the financial and political crisis facing the ancien régime. The announcement sparked widespread hope among the Third Estate that their grievances would be addressed, but it also alarmed the nobility, who feared losing their privileges. The convening of the Estates-General would set in motion the events that led to the French Revolution.
events.certainty_note: The announcement of the Estates-General is precisely documented in royal decrees and contemporary sources.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Battle of the Liman
events.description: In June 1788, Russian naval forces under Admiral Samuel Greig defeated Ottoman forces at the Battle of the Liman (the mouth of the Dnieper River on the Black Sea). The battle was a significant Russian naval victory and demonstrated Russian naval superiority in the Black Sea. The victory strengthened Russia's control of the northern Black Sea coast and contributed to the eventual Russian gains in the Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792). The battle also demonstrated the effectiveness of Russian naval technology and tactics, which had been modernised under Catherine II.

sources.name: Russian naval records and Greig's correspondence

events.certainty_note: The battle is documented in Russian and Ottoman sources. The date and outcome are well-established.

events.title: Austro-Turkish War begins
events.description: In 1788, Austria, allied with Russia, declared war on the Ottoman Empire. The Austro-Turkish War (1788–1791) was fought in the Balkans and resulted in Austrian gains in the region. The war was part of the broader conflict between the Ottoman Empire and the rising powers of Russia and Austria. The Austrian campaign was initially successful, with the capture of several Ottoman fortresses, but the war ultimately resulted in limited territorial gains for Austria. The war would be concluded by the Treaty of Sistova (1791), which granted Austria some territorial concessions but less than it had hoped. The Austro-Turkish War demonstrated the declining power of the Ottoman Empire and the rising power of Austria and Russia in the region.
key_figures: Catherine II (Russian Empress, ally of Austria)
events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Austro-Turkish War in 1788 is documented in Austrian and Ottoman sources.

events.title: First Fleet arrives in Port Jackson, establishing British colony in Australia
events.description: On 26 January 1788, the First Fleet, a convoy of British ships carrying convicts, soldiers, and supplies, arrived at Port Jackson (Sydney Cove) on the east coast of Australia. The fleet, commanded by Captain Arthur Phillip, established the first permanent European settlement in Australia. The settlement was established as a penal colony to relieve overcrowding in British prisons and to claim the territory for Britain. The arrival of the First Fleet marked the beginning of European colonisation of Australia and had profound consequences for the Indigenous Aboriginal peoples, who faced dispossession, disease, and violence. The establishment of the colony set in motion the process of European settlement and the eventual transformation of Australia into a British dominion.
key_figures: Captain Arthur Phillip (commander of the First Fleet)

sources.name: Arthur Phillip's journals and official reports
Ship logs and records of the First Fleet

events.certainty_note: The arrival of the First Fleet is precisely documented with the date of 26 January 1788. The event is extensively documented in British records.

events.title: Tipu Sultan launches Fourth Anglo-Mysore War
events.description: In 1788, Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore, launched the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1788–1799) by attacking British-allied territories in southern India. Tipu's military campaigns were driven by his ambition to modernise Mysore and resist British expansion. The war would last over a decade and ultimately result in Tipu's defeat and death in 1799. Tipu's forces were initially successful, but the British, under the command of Lord Cornwallis, mobilised superior resources and eventually forced Tipu into a defensive position. The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War was the longest and most costly of the Anglo-Mysore Wars and demonstrated the growing military superiority of the British East India Company.

sources.name: British East India Company records and military reports

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War in 1788 is documented, but some sources date the beginning to 1789. The year 1788 is assigned by convention.

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Macartney Embassy planned
events.description: In 1788, the British government began formal planning for the Macartney Embassy to China. The embassy was conceived as a major diplomatic initiative aimed at establishing formal relations with the Qing Dynasty and opening Chinese ports to British trade. The planning process involved consultations with merchants, diplomats, and scholars familiar with China. Lord George Macartney was selected as the ambassador, and preparations began for the journey. The embassy would depart in 1792 and arrive in China in 1793. The planning of the embassy reflected Britain's growing commercial interests in Asia and its ambitions to reshape the global trading system.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
events.certainty_note: The planning of the embassy is documented in British government records, but the exact date of its conception varies slightly in sources.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's reign at its height; internal challenges emerging
events.description: In 1788, the Qianlong Emperor, who had ruled the Qing Dynasty since 1735, was at the height of his power and prestige. The Qing Dynasty controlled vast territories and was the world's largest economy. However, by 1788, internal challenges were beginning to emerge: population growth was straining resources, corruption in the bureaucracy was increasing, and peasant unrest was growing. The White Lotus Rebellion (1796–1804) would soon erupt, signalling the beginning of the Qing Dynasty's decline. In 1788, the Qianlong Emperor was still confident in Qing superiority and rejected overtures from Western powers, including the planned Macartney Embassy. The year 1788 marked the beginning of the end of the Qing Dynasty's golden age.
key_figures: Heshen (Qianlong's favourite minister, source of corruption)
events.certainty_note: The Qianlong Emperor's reign and the emergence of internal challenges are documented in Qing records, but the exact dating of the beginning of decline is debated by historians.

events.title: British Parliament debates abolition of the slave trade
events.description: In 1788, William Wilberforce and other abolitionists introduced motions in the House of Commons to abolish the British slave trade. Although the motions were defeated, they marked the beginning of a sustained parliamentary campaign against slavery. The debates introduced evidence of the horrors of the slave trade and challenged the economic arguments in its favour. The abolitionist movement, which included Quakers, Evangelicals, and other religious groups, would continue to press for abolition throughout the 1790s and early 1800s. The Slave Trade Act would eventually be passed in 1807, ending British participation in the slave trade. The 1788 debates marked the beginning of the end of British slavery.
events.certainty_note: The 1788 parliamentary debates on abolition are documented in parliamentary records.

events.title: Madras Famine in southern India
events.description: In 1788, a severe famine struck the Madras Presidency in southern India, resulting in widespread death and suffering. The famine was caused by drought and crop failure, exacerbated by the disruptions of the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War. The British East India Company's policies, including the collection of taxes in kind and the disruption of trade, worsened the famine. Hundreds of thousands of people died from starvation and disease. The Madras Famine demonstrated the vulnerability of Indian agriculture to environmental shocks and the impact of British colonial policies on Indian society. The famine also highlighted the tensions between British commercial interests and the welfare of the Indian population.
key_figures: Indian peasants and farmers (victims of the famine)

sources.name: British East India Company records and reports

events.certainty_note: The Madras Famine of 1788 is documented in British East India Company records, but casualty figures and exact dates vary between sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The U.S. Constitution's ratification is sometimes portrayed as a triumph of Federalism, but the ratification process was contentious and the Constitution was adopted by narrow margins in several key states. The decision to convene the Estates-General is sometimes presented as a sign of the king's weakness, but Louis XVI may have hoped that the Estates-General would endorse his financial reforms and strengthen his authority. The First Fleet's arrival in Australia is sometimes celebrated as a triumph of British exploration and colonisation, but it resulted in the dispossession and decimation of Indigenous Aboriginal peoples. The Madras Famine is sometimes attributed solely to natural causes, but modern scholarship emphasises the role of British colonial policies in exacerbating the famine.
historiographic_note: The year 1788 is well-documented for Europe and North America, with rich archival sources for Britain, France, and the United States. Documentation of events in Asia, Africa, and the Ottoman Empire is thinner and often filtered through European sources. The First Fleet's arrival in Australia is extensively documented in British records but from a colonial perspective; Indigenous Aboriginal perspectives are largely absent from contemporary written records. The Madras Famine is documented in British East India Company records, but Indian perspectives are underrepresented. The year reflects the biases of surviving archives: government records, parliamentary proceedings, and published narratives dominate, while popular movements and non-elite perspectives are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The Constitution's ratification in 1788 enabled Washington's inauguration in 1789.
Louis XVI's decision to convene the Estates-General in 1788 led to its convening in 1789.
The First Fleet's arrival in 1788 established the foundation for British colonisation of Australia.
The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War begun in 1788 culminated in Tipu Sultan's death in 1799.
